mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive coverage of Japan from Upper Paleolithic settlement, feudal shogunates (Kamakura, Ashikaga, Tokugawa), and isolationism addresses the character of Japanese civilization (A), early Japan (B), and feudal Japan through the Tokugawa period (C).

Tang dynasty tributary relations with Japan and transmission of Chinese culture relate to Chinese influences on the Yamato court (B.2.a), Shotoku Taishi's relations with China (B.2.b), and Chinese and Indian influences during the Nara period (B.3.a).

China's dynastic history provides context for Japanese-Chinese cultural exchange (A), the influx of Chinese culture in early Japan (B.2), and increased trade with China during the Muromachi period (C.2.c).
The Mongol invasions of Japan in 1274 and 1281 and Japanese resistance directly address the Kamakura period's defense against Mongol attacks (C.1.c).
Genghis Khan's legacy through his successors' campaigns includes the Mongol invasions of Japan (C.1.c), though the actual invasions occurred under Kublai Khan.

South Korea's coverage of ancient Korean kingdoms (Goguryeo, Baekje, Silla) and unification under Goryeo and Joseon dynasties before Japanese annexation directly addresses Korea's history from prehistoric origins through the Yi dynasty (D).

East Asia as a geocultural region with Chinese civilizational influence on Japan and Korea, including Confucianism, Buddhism, and Shinto, provides context for the character of Japanese and Korean civilizations (A).

Confucian and Neo-Confucian thought influenced the Kamakura period (C.1.d), Tokugawa cultural developments including the Shinto revival and Confucianism (C.4.d.ii), and the establishment of a Confucian state in the Yi dynasty (D.5.a).

Zen Buddhism and Korean Neo-Confucianism as discussed in Eastern philosophy relate to Buddhist culture during the Kamakura period and Zen's influence (C.1.d) and the emergence of Confucian culture in Choson Korea (D.5.a).
The early modern period's coverage of Tokugawa Japan and Joseon Korea provides context for the period of unification and warring country (C.3) and Tokugawa military-bureaucratic rule (C.4).

The post-classical period (c. 500-1450) covers the Nara and Heian periods (B.3), Kamakura feudal era (C.1), Muromachi period (C.2), Korean Three Kingdoms (D.2), Silla unification (D.3), and Koryo dynasty (D.4).
Tokyo's evolution from the Edo fishing village to seat of the Tokugawa shogunate (1603) directly addresses the establishment of the Tokugawa Edo shogunate (C.4.a).

The arrival of the Portuguese (1543) and Spanish (1549) in Japan, opening of trade, and Catholic Jesuit missionary activity directly address European contact during the warring country period (C.3.b).
The Han dynasty's military expansion into the Korean Peninsula provides context for the prehistoric origins and early development of Korean civilization (D.1).
Bronze Age developments in East Asia relate to the emergence of tribal states and the use of ironware in Korea's Bronze Age (D.1).
